fragments.content: Hyponatremia is serum sodium <135 mEq/L, the most common electrolyte disorder in clinical practice. Classification by tonicity: hypotonic (true hyponatremia, most common), isotonic (pseudohyponatremia from hyperlipidemia/hyperproteinemia), hypertonic (hyperglycemia, mannitol). Hypotonic hyponatremia classified by volume status: (1) Hypovolemic - sodium and water loss (diuretics, GI losses, third-spacing, mineralocorticoid deficiency); (2) Euvolemic - normal total body sodium with excess water (SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, psychogenic polydipsia, thiazides); (3) Hypervolemic - increased total body sodium with greater water retention (heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure). Symptoms depend on severity and acuity: mild (nausea, malaise), moderate (headache, confusion, lethargy), severe (seizures, coma, respiratory arrest). Acute (<48 hours) more symptomatic than chronic due to cerebral edema.

Treatment based on severity, acuity, and volume status. Severe symptomatic (seizures, altered mental status): immediate 3% hypertonic saline 100-150mL IV bolus, repeat up to 3 times if needed; target increase 4-6 mEq/L in first few hours. Hypovolemic: isotonic saline to restore volume (suppresses ADH). Euvolemic (SIADH): fluid restriction (<800-1000mL/day), salt tablets, loop diuretics; vaptans (tolvaptan) for refractory cases; demeclocycline for chronic SIADH. Hypervolemic: fluid and sodium restriction, loop diuretics, treat underlying condition; vaptans in select cases. Correction rate critical: limit to 6-8 mEq/L in first 24 hours, <18 mEq/L in 48 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS). Higher risk of ODS if alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease, hypokalemia. If overcorrection occurs, use DDAVP and D5W to re-lower sodium. Chronic asymptomatic: gradual correction with fluid restriction, address underlying cause.

Diagnostic algorithm: (1) Confirm hypotonicity: serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg (excludes pseudohyponatremia, hypertonic causes); (2) Assess volume status clinically (orthostasis, JVP, edema, skin turgor); (3) Measure urine osmolality: <100 mOsm/kg suggests appropriate ADH suppression (psychogenic polydipsia, low solute intake); >100 indicates ADH present; (4) Measure urine sodium: <30 mEq/L suggests hypovolemia (except diuretics, adrenal insufficiency) or hypervolemia; >30 mEq/L suggests SIADH, renal salt wasting, diuretics, mineralocorticoid deficiency. SIADH diagnostic criteria: euvolemia, serum osmolality <275, urine osmolality >100, urine Na >30-40, normal thyroid/adrenal/renal function, no diuretics. Additional tests: TSH, morning cortisol, uric acid (low in SIADH), BUN/creatinine. Calculate corrected sodium for hyperglycemia: add 1.6-2.4 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose >100.
